data_quality: v0.1 best-effort population from cited public sources. Each entity's compat claims trace to source_ids on its attributes. Verification pass required before production launch; this dataset is sized to prove the engine end-to-end, not for general consumer trust.

sources.name: keebfol.io
sources.scope: Community-maintained mechanical keyboard component database. Used to confirm switch/keycap/plate/PCB compatibility, mount style, layout coverage, and stab type. Sanctioned in the crosspec project CLAUDE.md as a non-adversarial open dataset.

sources.name: r/MechanicalKeyboards wiki + buying guides
sources.scope: Community-maintained wiki used to confirm popular kit/switch/keycap/plate combinations and buying-guide consensus on common compatibility pitfalls (5-pin sockets, plate-mount vs PCB-mount stabs, ANSI vs ISO keycap kits).

sources.name: Manufacturer + retailer public product pages
sources.scope: Used to confirm vendor-stated specs (layout, switch_mount, firmware, materials, weight, price). Includes Drop, KBDfans, NovelKeys, Cannonkeys, Mode Designs, Glorious, Keychron, Akko, ZSA, and Wuque Studio public product pages.

attributes.note: The reference 60% aluminum DIY case. Tray-mount, takes a standard 60% PCB + plate. Heavy CNC body for a fraction of custom-group-buy money.
Gasket-mount 65% with hotswap PCB. Ships ready to build — the safest 65% entry kit if you don't want to solder.
Mode Designs' premium 65%. Multi-mount system (o-ring stock, leaf and tadpole sold separately). PVD weight options and modular feet.
The most popular 'first custom' kit. Gasket-mount, hotswap, full QMK/VIA — ships with screw-in stabs already installed.
65% sibling of the Q1. Same gasket-mount build, same QMK/VIA support — just a smaller footprint and lighter wallet hit.
TKL Q-series. If you need function row + nav cluster but not numpad, this is the sweet spot.
96% layout — full numpad without the dead space of true 100%. Same gasket build as the rest of the Q line.
True 100% with full numpad cluster. Heaviest Q-series stock — three pounds of CNC aluminum.
Alice ergonomic layout in Q-series build. Note the split spacebar — most generic keycap kits don't include the right spacebars; check for an Alice/ergo kit.
Alice + macro/nav column on the left. Even rarer keycap fit — confirm the kit covers macro column and split bar before buying.
Wireless 75% with QMK/VIA support. Tray-mount plastic — feels worse than the Q-series but adds Bluetooth and is half the price.
TKL wireless with hotswap and QMK/VIA. The cheapest wireless TKL with VIA support.
Wired-only V-series TKL. Same QMK/VIA software as the Q-series for under $100. Tray-mount plastic, hotswap PCB.
Glorious's flagship 75%. Modular o-ring/gasket mount, brass weight, rotary encoder. QMK/VIA after firmware switch from stock.
Budget hotswap 96%. Plastic build, but gets you VIA + a numpad cheaper than most options.
Original GMMK TKL. Hotswap, plastic case, Glorious-branded software (no QMK). The cheap hotswap TKL benchmark.
Cheapest hotswap 60% from a major brand. Plastic case, vendor software (no QMK).
The classic 75% DIY kit. Aluminum tray-mount case, hotswap PCB, QMK/VIA. Long-running KBDfans staple.
Aluminum TKL DIY kit. Hotswap PCB sold separately as an option. Solid budget TKL canvas for builds.
Gasket-mount premium TKL with brass weight. Quieter, more flex than the KBD87. Higher-end Cherry stab support — screw-in only.
40% aluminum case. Many 'standard' keycap sets do NOT cover 40% layouts — confirm the kit ships with 1u alphas, no number row, and short spacebar coverage.
Akko's wireless gasket TKL. Cherry-clone stabs pre-installed, hotswap, ships with Akko switches. Vendor software only — no QMK.
Wired full-size with hotswap. Cheap entry into Akko's ecosystem with their CS / V3 switches. No QMK.
Premium 75% with multi-mount system (leaf, o-ring, tadpole). Modular weights and accent pieces. The pre-built that 'feels custom'.
TKL flagship from Mode. Same multi-mount system as the Sonnet. PVD finishes and modular weights — high-end builder territory.
60% Tofu in HHKB layout — split right shift, split backspace. Most ANSI keycap sets work with the right kit; double-check for the 1u right-shift cap.
Split ergo with columnar staggering. All keys 1u — most standard keycap sets won't fit. Use a DSA/XDA set or a 40s-friendly Cherry kit with enough 1u alphas.
Split ergo with OLED + rotary encoder. Open-source PCB, sold by multiple vendors. All 1u keys plus 1.5u thumb caps — niche keycap requirement.
Split ergo, fully assembled with tilt/tent legs and wrist rest. ZSA's Oryx web configurator on top of QMK. Keycap selection is sparse — most builds use blank or ZSA's DCS set.
Split ergo with adjustable thumb cluster and tenting. ZSA Oryx web configurator. Like the ErgoDox EZ, keycap options are limited to columnar-friendly sets.
Low-profile split. Uses Kailh Choc switches ONLY — Cherry MX switches and standard MX keycaps will not fit. Treat this as a totally separate ecosystem from MX builds.
Premium Alice / split ergo. Soldered PCB, gasket-mount. Niche keycap fit — split spacebar means most generic kits won't work.
12x4 ortholinear 40%. All keys 1u — DSA, XDA, MT3 /dev/tty, or KAT Lich blanks are typical fits. Generic Cherry kits will leave most blank.
Ortholinear 5x12. Same keycap restriction as the Planck — 1u only. Often paired with KAT Lich, MT3 /dev/tty, or DSA Drifter.
Cheapest 40% kit. Soldered PCB, plastic tray-mount. Standard staggered (not ortho), so any 40s-friendly Cherry kit with 1u alphas works.
Premium wireless TKL with low-profile GL switches. Proprietary keycaps — no aftermarket fit. Closed ecosystem; included only as a baseline 'mainstream wireless' option.
Razer's analog optical TKL. Cherry-style stems and stab cutout, so MX keycaps fit. No QMK; Razer Synapse only.
The reference MX linear. Light, smooth, scratchy out of the box. 3-pin — fits any MX socket whether 3 or 5-pin. Lube before building if you care about smoothness.
Mild bump tactile. Often described as a 'linear with hair'. Standard 3-pin MX. Most prebuilt 'tactile' keyboards ship with these or a clone.
Click-jacket clicky. Loud — disliked in most office and gaming contexts. Standard 3-pin MX.
Heavy linear. Stiffer than Reds. Vintage variants ('Nixies') from older boards are a hot collector item.
Silent linear with rubber dampers in the stem. Slightly mushy bottom-out. Best Cherry option for a quiet office build.
Short-throw linear — 1.2 mm actuation. Marketed for gaming. Easier to mistype on; not recommended for touch typists.
The cheap 'enthusiast pick' linear. Smooth out of the box; gold standard for budget builds. 5-pin — clip the extra legs to fit 3-pin sockets.
Cherry MX Brown clone — slightly smoother. Same mild-tactile feel. Often the budget 'tactile' default.
Lighter than Yellow. Similar Cherry MX Red replacement at half the price.
Heavy linear MX clone. Smoother than vintage Cherry Blacks but lighter character.
Factory-lubed Yellow. Smoother out of box, no need to lube manually. The de facto 'best budget pre-lubed' linear.
Factory-lubed heavy linear with INK-style housing. Deep, marbley sound signature. One of the 'safe pick' premium linears.
Strong-tactile 'D-shaped' bump. Heavier and snappier than Browns. One of the cleanest premium tactiles available off-the-shelf.
Click-bar clicky, dust/water resistant. Cleaner click than Cherry Blue. The community favorite for clicky builds.
Heavier sibling of Box White with thicker click bar. Loud, deep click. Iconic typewriter-on-meds sound.
Low-profile Choc V1 mount. NOT MX-compatible — needs Choc-spaced PCB and Choc keycaps. Used in ZSA Voyager and many low-profile splits.
Factory-lubed low-profile linear. Great for the Voyager and Sweep family. MX hardware and keycaps will not fit Choc layouts.
Cheapest enthusiast-tier factory-lubed linear. Pink translucent housing, clacky sound. The 'tape mod and wow' switch on a budget.
Light tactile, rounded D-bump. Holy-Panda-adjacent feel for under $0.30 a switch.
Smoother, deeper Akko linear. Marbley sound profile, factory lubed. Great drop-in for prebuilts that came with Browns.
Premium tactile with strong, rounded bump. Zeal's flagship tactile. Hand-lube recommended for the cleanest possible feel.
Premium linear sibling of Zealios V2. Smooth, heavy, polycarbonate top. The 'price ceiling' linear for many builders before lubed Gaterons closed the gap.
Drop's mass-market Holy Panda. The 'rounded D-shaped tactile bump' that started the modern tactile craze. Lube before building.
Glorious's Holy Panda alternative. Rounded tactile bump. Cheaper than Drop's HP but slightly lighter feel.
Cult-classic thocky tactile. D-shaped strong bump and proprietary housing for deeper sound. Bottlenecked supply — verify availability before quoting.
Sub-$0.20 linear. The cheapest factory-lubed switch worth recommending. Quality-control variance is higher than Gateron.
Proprietary 'Lyrium' stem. Smooth, deep, slightly poppy sound. Tecsee's headline linear.
Wuque Studio's mid-weight linear. Marbled top housing for distinct sound. Premium boutique alternative to Pro Yellow.
Refined Drop Holy Panda iteration. Cleaner bump, less wobble than the original.
Box-stem linear. Dust/water resistant. Slightly shorter travel than standard MX. 3-pin.
Box-stem tactile. Sharper bump than Cherry MX Brown. Dust/water resistant.
Cap V2 series with milky housing. Deeper, marbley sound. Factory-lubed. Often picked over Pro Yellow for thockier builds.
All-POM housing and stem. Heavy linear, distinct deep sound. Self-lubricating polymer means it stays smooth without lube.
Long-running 'safe pick' premium linear. Balanced weight, smooth stock, takes lube well.
Light, smooth box linear. Clear housing for RGB shine-through. 3-pin.
Mid-weight, factory-lubed linear. Often called the 'cleanest under $0.70'. Solid daily driver.
Light silent linear. Best-in-class quietness — community favorite for libraries / bedrooms / shared offices.
Iconic INK housing — semi-transparent, deeper sound. Heavier classic linear. Pairs well with brass plates for thock.
All-POM cult linear. Famously scratchy out of box but smooths out after break-in. Distinct creamy sound.
Doubleshot ABS Cherry profile in pink/cream. Iconic enthusiast set. Covers most ANSI/ISO and split spacebar layouts.
Blue and white doubleshot ABS Cherry. Still in regular re-runs. Solid all-rounder ANSI/ISO coverage.
Red/cream Cherry-profile ABS. R2 added Alice/ergo kit — one of the few GMK sets that covers split spacebar properly.
Submarine/Jules-Verne theme. Teal with orange accents. Standard ANSI/ISO + tsangan; no Alice.
Apollo astronaut theme. Best layout coverage of any GMK set — covers Alice and 40s out of the box.
Doubleshot PBT Cherry profile. The 'PBT alternative to GMK' for clean monochrome builds. Standard ANSI/ISO only.
Doubleshot PBT in Beeswarm yellow. Same kit coverage as BoW. ANSI-friendly.
ASA profile (Akko's own — between OEM and SA). Dye-sub PBT. Best budget keycap value — full layout coverage for $59.
Vintage IBM 9009 inspired Cherry-profile PBT. Among the cheapest 'looks expensive' sets. Standard layouts only.
MT3 sculpted profile, doubleshot ABS. Mostly blank caps — works on 40s, ortho, and ergo layouts where standard sets fail.
MT3 terminal-green. Excellent ortho/40s coverage. Cult favorite for Planck and Preonic builds.
MT3 monochrome. Sculpted profile gives a typewriter feel. Heavy Alice + tsangan kit coverage.
KAT profile (uniform-ish sculpt, between SA and DSA). One of the broadest layout-coverage sets — works on splits, ortho, 40s, alice.
Cyberpunk-themed Cherry doubleshot ABS. In-stock alternative to GMK for sci-fi builds.
JTK's GMK alternative — Cherry profile ABS doubleshot, slightly cheaper. ANSI/ISO/tsangan covered.
Pink cherry-blossom on navy. Solid coverage for ANSI 60 through 100. No Alice/40s.
Galaxy-print Cherry-profile PBT. KBDfans in-house — typically in stock. Budget pick for purple/space themes.
OSA profile (Keychron's spherical OEM-height variant). Wide kit — covers Alice and most layouts. Good default for Q-series builds.
ASA profile dye-sub PBT. ANSI-only; no ISO. Cheap, durable office build option.
XDA uniform profile, all keys flat-top. PBT dye-sub. The 'works on anything' set — splits, ortho, 40s included.
DSA flat uniform profile, doubleshot ABS. Often used for ortho/split builds where Cherry profile makes typing weird.
KAT-profile teal/white. Wide layout coverage. Alternate 'safe pick' KAT set.
Choc-spaced low-profile keycaps. Will NOT fit MX switches or MX-spaced PCBs. Designed for the Voyager / Sweep / Corne low-profile family.
Sculpted Choc-spaced PBT. Choc-only — no MX compatibility. MCC mimics Cherry profile in low-profile form.
ISO-compatible OSA profile. Includes Nordic and German ISO sublegends. Default fit for ISO Q-series builds.
Green/cream botanical theme. Standard ANSI/ISO. No Alice or 40s coverage.
Pastel KAT profile. Same broad-layout-coverage strategy as KAT Lich/Atlantis.
PCB-mount screw-in. Wire pre-bent with reduced rattle. The default 'enthusiast' stab. Will not fit plate-mount-only PCBs.
Original Cherry screw-in. PCB-mount only. Lube needed for clean sound. Reliable but cheaper clones (Durock V2) often outperform.
Plate-mount Cherry stabilizers. Works with most pre-built kits but won't fit a screw-in PCB. Common in older budget kits.
Older Costar-style stabs — exposed metal wire, snap-in plastic inserts. Need a Costar-cutout PCB. Most modern PCBs are Cherry-cutout only.
Premium PCB-mount screw-in. Tight-fitting wires reduce rattle. Pricey for what they do — Durock V2 is competitive at 70% the price.
Boutique-tier PCB-mount screw-ins. Tightest tolerances on the market. Builders love them; price reflects scarcity.
Updated Durock V3 with cleaner wire bend and revised housing. Drop-in replacement for V2 — same PCB-mount screw-in fit.
Stock Keychron screw-in stabs. Decent out of box, hand-lube to upgrade. Already installed on Q-series kits.
Pre-lubed screw-in PCB-mount stabs. Decent stock fit; included by default in some GMMK Pro builds.
Gold-plated wire screw-in stabs. Smooth wire travel out of box. Popular budget alternative to Durock.
Brass 60% plate. Stiffer feel and brighter sound than alu. Fits most standard 60% PCBs cut to Cherry stab cutouts.
Standard alu 60% plate. Default 'reference' feel. Cheap, plentiful.
Polycarbonate 60% plate. Soft, deep feel. Pairs well with linears for that 'marbley' sound.
POM 60% plate. Softer than PC, slightly snappier than carbon fiber. Niche builder pick.
FR4 (PCB material) 60% plate. Mid-flex, relatively cheap. Good first 'try a non-alu plate' upgrade.
Carbon fiber 60% plate. Stiff with a clacky high-pitched sound. Cosmetically distinctive.
Stock Q1 V2 plate. Aluminum, optimized for the gasket mount. Drop-in fit only — won't work outside Q1 V2.
PC plate for Q1 V2. Softer typing feel and deeper sound on the gasket-mounted Q1.
Stock TKL plate for Keychron Q3. Drop-in only. Aluminum, gasket-compatible cutouts.
Stock alu plate for KBD75 V3. Drop-in only — KBD75-specific cut.
Brass TKL plate for KBD87. Stiff, bright sound. KBD87-specific cutout.
PC plate for GMMK Pro. Softer, deeper feel than the stock brass. GMMK Pro-specific.
Stock brass plate for GMMK Pro. Bright pingy sound; many builders swap to PC or alu.
PC plate for KBD8X MK3. Pairs with the gasket mount for a soft typing feel.
Pair of alu plates (one per half) for the Iris. Iris-specific cutouts. Kit ships with PCB-only or PCB+plate variants.
Aluminum plate for the Planck ortholinear. Planck-specific 4x12 ortho cut.
POM plate for the Preonic 5x12 ortho. Soft typing feel. Preonic-specific.

entities.name: Mech Cables Coiled USB-C to USB-A (5 ft)

attributes.note: Coiled paracord-sleeved USB-C to USB-A cable. Compatible with most kits. Pure aesthetic upgrade — function identical to a flat cable.
Coiled aviator cable with detachable connector. Cheaper than boutique mech cables. Compatible with any USB-C kit.
Drop-branded coiled aviator cable. Multiple colorway drops. Compatible with any USB-C kit.
Standard braided USB-C cable. Ships with most Keychron Q/V/K-series kits. Cheap drop-in.
Coiled aviator cable available in many sleeve colors. Detachable aviator connector lets you swap cables without unplugging from the kit.
Soldered 60% PCB. The reference DIY 60% board. Fits any standard 60% case. ANSI / ISO / HHKB / split-spacebar layouts all supported via firmware.
Hotswap 60% PCB with RGB. 5-pin sockets — clip 5-pin switches if needed. Same 60% standard case fit as DZ60.
75% hotswap PCB. KBD75-specific cutout. Includes Cherry-cut stab support and 5-pin sockets.
Soldered TKL PCB. KBD8X MK3-specific. Screw-in stab cutouts only — won't accept plate-mount stabs.
Stock Q1 V2 PCB. ANSI/ISO variants. Built-in screw-in stab cutouts. Drop-in only — won't fit other kits.
Stock Q3 TKL PCB with screw-in stab support. Drop-in only.
Stock GMMK Pro PCB. Hotswap, RGB, rotary encoder. Originally vendor firmware, now QMK-supported.
Pair of split PCBs for the Iris. Hotswap. Rev 6 added per-key RGB. USB-C with TRRS link cable between halves.
Latest Planck PCB. Hotswap, USB-C. Drop-in for Planck cases / EZ. 4x12 ortho only.
Through-hole DIY ortho PCB. Mini-USB, ATmega328. Hobbyist soldering project. Niche — only mention for ortho fans.
Acrylic case stack with DZ60-compatible PCB. Cheap entry-level 60% project. RGB underglow shines through the frosted acrylic.
ISO variant of the Q3 PCB. Same kit fit. ANSI keycap kits will not fit — pick an ISO-compatible keycap set.
Wireless QMK-capable PCB. Bluetooth + 2.4GHz + wired tri-mode. Drop-in for K2 Pro only.
Soldered KBD75 PCB. Cheaper than hotswap, requires soldering kit. Same case fit.

then.pcb.attributes.switch_mount == 'hotswap': must accept 5-pin OR user must clip 5-pin switches to 3-pin

constraints.rationale: 5-pin switches have two extra plastic legs for plate-less stabilization. They fit 5-pin PCBs natively. They fit 3-pin sockets only after clipping the extra legs (irreversible).

A standard ANSI 60/65/75/TKL keycap set will not have the right caps for HHKB split right-shift, Alice split spacebar, 40% or ortholinear layouts, or low-profile Choc spacing. Kit's pcb_form_factor must be in the keycap set's layout_compat.
Kailh Choc switches and Choc keycaps use a different stem and key spacing. Choc keycaps will not fit MX switches; MX keycaps will not fit Choc switches. Treat MX and Choc as separate ecosystems.

then.pcb.attributes.layout: must have screw-in stab cutouts (every modern Cherry-cut PCB does; older Costar PCBs don't)

constraints.rationale: PCB-mount screw-in stabilizers need clearance holes in the PCB to thread through. Plate-mount stabilizers clip into a plate cutout and don't touch the PCB. Older Costar-cut PCBs accept Costar stabs only.

Kits that ship with screw-in stabilizers (or have Cherry-screw-in cutouts) won't accept plate-mount or Costar stabs. Vice versa is also true — older plate-mount kits won't accept screw-in stabs without modding.
Plates are cut to the exact PCB / kit footprint. A KBD75 V3 plate doesn't fit a Keychron Q1 even though both are 75%. Always match plate.compatible_kits to the chosen kit id.

Vendor-proprietary firmware (Glorious software, Razer Synapse, Logitech G Hub, vendor Akko software) cannot be replaced with QMK/VIA without ROM-level reflashing — for most users that means it's effectively impossible. If the user wants QMK or VIA, every other PCB choice must be excluded.

PCBs are case-specific. The KBD75 V3 PCB will not fit a Keychron Q1 V2 even though they're both 75%. The DZ60 fits most generic 60% cases (Tofu60, GMMK 60), but vendor-specific cases like the Keychron Q-series take only their own PCB.

then.keycap_set.attributes.layout_compat: must include 'iso_uk' or relevant ISO variant

constraints.rationale: ISO PCBs use a wider left shift, taller flat enter, and an extra alpha key. ANSI-only keycap sets can't fill these positions correctly.

If the user explicitly needs quiet, silent linears/tactiles (Cherry MX Silent Red, Kailh Silent Box Pink) are the only switches that meaningfully drop the noise floor. Standard linears/tactiles are still loud at bottom-out even with lube and dampening.

goals.prompt: Every selected entity (kit + switches + keycaps + plate + stabs + cable + PCB) must pass every applicable constraint above.
Prefer combinations that don't require switch clipping, plate cutting, firmware flashing, or stab modding to fit.
If the user is a first-time builder, prefer factory-lubed switches and stabs to avoid the lube-and-film step.
